questions.question: A dataset contains five integers: 12, 18, 15, x, and 20. If the median equals the mean, what is the value of x?
questions.explanation: The mean is (12 + 18 + 15 + x + 20)/5 = (65 + x)/5. When ordered, the median is the middle value. For median = mean, we need (65 + x)/5 to equal the third value when sorted. Testing x = 15 gives mean = 16 and sorted list {12, 15, 15, 18, 20} with median 15. Wait—let me recalculate: if x = 15, mean = 80/5 = 16. The median of {12, 15, 15, 18, 20} is 15, not 16. Setting (65 + x)/5 = median requires solving by cases. If x ≤ 12, median = 15 and mean = (65 + x)/5 = 15 gives x = 10 (check: works). If 12 < x ≤ 15, median = x and x = (65 + x)/5 gives 5x = 65 + x, so x = 16.25 (contradiction). If x > 15, median = 18 and 18 = (65 + x)/5 gives x = 25. Verify x = 10: {10, 12, 15, 18, 20}, median 15, mean 15. ✓

questions.question: Four test scores have a mean of 82 and a standard deviation of 6. If a fifth score of 100 is added, by approximately how much does the standard deviation change? Round to the nearest tenth.
questions.explanation: Original data: mean = 82, σ ≈ 6, n = 4. Sum = 328. Adding 100: new sum = 428, new mean = 428/5 = 85.6. Original variance = 36. New variance = [(82−82)² + (82−82)² + (82−82)² + (82−82)² + (100−85.6)²]/5... Actually, compute deviations from new mean: (−3.6)² × 4 terms from original + (14.4)² = 51.84 + 207.36 = 259.2/5 = 51.84. So new σ ≈ 7.2. Change = 7.2 − 6 = 1.2. [Recalculating with original spread: variance was 36; new data has larger spread, σ increases to ~7.2, change ≈ +1.2.]

questions.question: In a dataset of 10 test scores, the interquartile range (IQR) is 12. If the first quartile Q₁ = 65 and the median = 75, what is the third quartile Q₃?
questions.explanation: IQR = Q₃ − Q₁. Given IQR = 12 and Q₁ = 65, we have 12 = Q₃ − 65, so Q₃ = 77. (The median = 75 is consistent but doesn't directly constrain Q₃; IQR definition alone determines it.)

questions.question: A professor records quiz scores for a class. The mean is 76, the median is 80, and there are no repeated scores. If one student's score of 92 is removed, the new median becomes 78. How many students are in the class?
questions.explanation: With n students, the median is the middle value(s). Removing a high score (92) lowers the median from 80 to 78, suggesting 92 was above the median. If n is odd, the median is the ((n+1)/2)th value. With 92 present and median 80, if 92 is the highest (or among the highest), removing it shifts the median down. After removal, n−1 students have median at position ((n−1)+1)/2 = n/2. For this to equal the (n−1)th value when originally there were n, testing n = 11: original median is the 6th value (= 80). Removing 92 leaves 10 students; new median is between 5th and 6th values. If the 6th value is 78, this works. Thus n = 11.

questions.question: A dataset has a mean of 50 and standard deviation of 8. If every data point is multiplied by 2 and then 10 is subtracted, what are the new mean and standard deviation?
questions.explanation: Transformation: new value = 2(old value) − 10. Linear transformations affect mean the same way: new mean = 2(50) − 10 = 90. Standard deviation depends only on scaling factor (not additive shifts), so new σ = 2(8) = 16.

questions.question: A club has 15 members with an average age of 28 years. Two new members aged 35 and 41 join. What is the new average age, rounded to the nearest tenth?
questions.explanation: Original total age = 15 × 28 = 420. After adding 35 and 41: new total = 420 + 35 + 41 = 496. New count = 17 members. New average = 496/17 ≈ 29.176 ≈ 29.2 years. [Double-check: 496 ÷ 17 = 29.176..., rounds to 29.2.]
